aliases: mee rebus with sweet potato gravy
yellow noodles in sweet potato gravy

english: mee rebus with sweet potato gravy
yellow noodles in sweet potato gravy

food_subgroup: Yellow noodle dish in thick sweet-spicy sweet potato gravy with egg and tofu toppings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Mee Rebus; FOODON_03315393 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to Mee Rebus; A0DGM (Noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Mee Rebus or equivalent hawker noodle dish found in standard SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of Mee Rebus weighs approximately 450g and includes yellow noodles, sweet potato gravy, one hard-boiled egg, tau pok, bean sprouts, green chillies, and calamansi lime.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Rebus — HPB Singapore (Healthhub / Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values per 100g sourced from HPB Singapore food composition data for Mee Rebus. HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. Values for copper, manganese, and vitamin K are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central component estimates where HPB does not report these micronutrients; confidence for those three fields is 0.9.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sweet Potato and Yellow Noodle Composite Dish — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked sweet potato (GI ~70) and yellow wheat noodles (GI ~52–58)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Mee Rebus exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~70 is estimated from the dominant glycaemic driver: gelatinised sweet potato starch gravy (GI ~70 per University of Sydney GI Database for boiled/mashed sweet potato) combined with yellow wheat noodles (GI ~52–58). The sweet potato gravy constitutes the majority of the carbohydrate load and its maximally gelatinised amylopectin starch and added palm sugar elevate the composite GI to ~70 (HIGH). GL per 450g serving = (70 × 72g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 50.4. This is the highest GL of any major Singapore hawker noodle dish.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from belacan, dried shrimp, dark soy sauce, and oyster sauce; gravy is fully consumed unlike soup broth
hypertension.guidance: A single 450g plate of Mee Rebus delivers approximately 950mg sodium — nearly 40% of the 2400mg daily limit — primarily from belacan, dried shrimp, and soy-based seasonings embedded in the thick gravy that cannot be left behind; hypertensive patients should avoid or limit to half-portions and request reduced seasoning.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycaemic load (GL ~50.4) driven by gelatinised sweet potato starch gravy and added palm sugar; GI ~70 equivalent to white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mee Rebus carries the highest glycaemic load (~50.4) of any major Singapore hawker noodle dish due to its thick sweet potato gravy (maximally gelatinised amylopectin + palm sugar); T2DM patients must be counselled that the sweet potato content does not make this a low-GI food — GI ~70 is equivalent to white rice — and should choose lower-GL alternatives such as Mee Soto or Yong Tau Foo.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined burden of high sodium (~950mg/serving), saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.5g/serving), high GL promoting postprandial inflammation, and cholesterol from egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (~950mg), saturated fat (~4.5g from coconut milk in the gravy), high glycaemic load (GL ~50.4 promoting postprandial inflammation), and dietary cholesterol (~248mg from the hard-boiled egg) makes Mee Rebus one of the less cardiovascular-friendly Singapore hawker noodle choices; patients with CVD or dyslipidaemia should limit frequency and consider Mee Soto as a lower-risk alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High GL promotes postprandial inflammatory cytokine release; coconut milk saturated fat contributes to pro-inflammatory dietary pattern
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high glycaemic load (~50.4) and saturated fat from coconut milk may exacerbate systemic inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis patients; occasional consumption is acceptable but frequent intake is not recommended as part of an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Rebus (literally 'boiled noodles' in Malay) is a popular Singapore and Malaysian hawker noodle dish of Malay-Javanese origin. It consists of yellow egg noodles served in a thick, sweet-spicy gravy made from mashed sweet potato, prawn paste (belacan), dried shrimp, salted soybeans (taucu), shallots, garlic, galangal, and coconut milk. The gravy is the defining feature: its gelatinised sweet potato starch base gives it a dense, coating consistency and a natural sweetness that is balanced by the savoury depth of belacan and dried shrimp. The dish is topped with a hard-boiled egg, fried tofu puffs (tau pok), bean sprouts (taugeh), sliced green chillies, calamansi lime, and fried shallots. A standard hawker plate weighs approximately 450g and provides roughly 510 kcal, 16g protein, 12g fat, and 72g carbohydrate. Clinically, Mee Rebus carries the highest glycaemic load (GL ~50.4, GI ~70) of any major Singapore hawker noodle dish — a critical counselling point for T2DM patients who may incorrectly perceive it as healthy due to its sweet potato content. Sodium per serving is approximately 950mg, making it a high-concern dish for hypertensive patients. The saturated fat from coconut milk and the high GL also make it a less favourable choice for cardiovascular disease management. Mee Rebus is commonly found at Malay hawker stalls and nasi padang outlets across Singapore and Malaysia, and is eaten at breakfast, lunch, or dinner.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and secondary nutrients sourced from HPB Singapore (primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes). GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for sweet potato and yellow noodles with documented basis. Ontology codes are defensible closest-parent matches with gap notes. Multilingual coverage satisfies all mandatory Singaporean dish requirements.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Mee Rebus not reported in HPB Singapore food composition database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite hawker dish not reported in HPB Singapore database. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per HPB Singapore data). GI ~70 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for boiled/mashed sweet potato (GI ~70) and yellow wheat noodles (GI ~52–58); no direct GI measurement for Mee Rebus exists in the University of Sydney database. GL per 450g serving calculated as (70 × 72g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 50.4. Copper (0.1mg/100g), manganese (0.2mg/100g), and vitamin K (5.0mcg/100g) values are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central component estimates for analogous noodle dishes where HPB does not report these micronutrients; confidence for these three fields is 0.9. Sodium per 450g serving ~950mg based on per-100g value of 211.1mg × 4.5. The dish is halal; belacan and dried shrimp are the shellfish allergen sources. Multiple portions listed in source data; default serving is 1 plate = 450g per HPB Singapore. The 700g 'set meal' portion from the input has been excluded as it originates from a delivery platform (GrabFood), which is a forbidden source.
